channel.title: Canadian Research Watch
channel.description: Recent academic research by Canadian-affiliated scholars or about Canada, generated from OpenAlex metadata.

item.title: Too Much to Review? When Production Outpaces Consumption
item.description:
Authors: Félix Arndt, Xin Zhang

Source: Canadian Journal of Administrative Sciences / Revue Canadienne des Sciences de l Administration

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related source title

Abstract: ABSTRACT The peer review system has often been characterized as broken. The adoption of generative AI has led to a steep increase in submissions that exposes a structural imbalance beneath it. We argue that this imbalance is not technological in nature, but reflects a deeper misalignment that performativity has brought to scholarly work. We offer a framework of AI‐mediated production and consumption of scholarly work, diagnose the effects of AI on the peer review system, and propose five recommendations to realign the role of human judgment in the process of peer evaluation.

item.title: Investigation of Ligand-to-Ligand Charge Transfer in Heteroleptic Zinc(II) 4'-Substituted 2,2':6',2''-Terpyridine Complexes
item.description:
Authors: Joseph Ricardo-Noordberg, Kiersten A. Hiebert, Taylorjade Layne, Adrien Donatini, Marek Majewski

Source: Canadian Journal of Chemistry

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related source title

Abstract: Molecular photosensitizers can use a donor-chromophore-acceptor architecture to achieve long-lived charge separated states that may more efficiently initiate the single electron transfer processes involved in photocatalysis or photovoltaic devices. These photosensitizers are typically metal complexes wherein the dominant lowest energy photon-absorbing state is a metal-to-ligand charge transfer, having molar absorptivities on the order of 10^4 cm^-1 M^-1, and charge separation is achieved through careful redox leveling of ancillary electron donating and accepting groups. Taking advantage of direct ligand-to-ligand charge transfer states introduces new excited state pathways that can also lead to charge separated states. In this work, we present a series of heteroleptic zinc(II) 2,2':6',2''-terpyridine photosensitizers containing a pendant pyridyl group and carbazole or diphenylamine...

item.title: Airway Complications After Cervical Spine Surgery: A Focused Review on Recent Evidence and Perioperative Management
item.description:
Authors: Ammar Siddiqui, Jason Chui

Source: Journal of Neurosurgical Anesthesiology

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canadian institution term detected

Abstract: Airway complications after cervical spine surgery are infrequent but potentially catastrophic. The mechanisms of postoperative airway compromise differ according to surgical approach, extent of surgery, and underlying cervical pathology and include prevertebral and airway soft-tissue edema, retropharyngeal hematoma, recurrent laryngeal nerve dysfunction, cervical malalignment, and restricted cervical mobility. In patients with concomitant cervical spinal cord injury or myelopathy, impaired respiratory mechanics and bulbar dysfunction may further increase airway vulnerability. Recent evidence suggests that postoperative airway vulnerability is dynamic rather than confined to the immediate perioperative period, with clinically significant respiratory and swallowing complications occurring days to weeks after surgery, including some after hospital discharge. This focused review summarizes...

item.title: Muscle Satellite Cells and Aging
item.description:
Authors: AL Johnson, Ryan T. Bevington, Samuel Rumelski, Natasha Kassamali, Gianni Parise

Source: American Journal of Physiology-Cell Physiology

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canadian institution term detected

Abstract: Aging is associated with a progressive decline in skeletal muscle mass and function, contributing to reduced physical capacity in older adults. Central is the deterioration of satellite cells, our tissue-resident muscle stem cells, which participate in adaptation, repair, and regeneration of skeletal muscle. Evidence from in vitro, murine, and human studies indicates an age-related reduction in satellite cell content, notably within type II muscle fibers, alongside impairments in myogenic potential. There is no single causative mechanism behind satellite cell age-related dysfunction, but a convergence of morphological changes and intrinsic and extrinsic factors that affect satellite cell dynamics and its niche. Intrinsic factors such as signalling pathways, cellular senescence, impaired autophagy, mitochondrial dysfunction, and epigenetic modifications can impact satellite cell...

item.title: Competing speech streams are simultaneously represented in the human cortex during attention switching
item.description:
Authors: Sara Carta, Emina Aličković, Johannes Zaar, Alejandro López Valdés, Giovanni M. Di Liberto

Source: PLoS Biology

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canadian institution term detected

Abstract: Successful speech communication in multi-talker scenarios requires a skillful combination of sustained attention and rapid attention switching. While the neurophysiology literature offers detailed insights into the neural underpinnings of sustained attention, there remains considerable uncertainty on how attention switching takes place. In this study, using EEG recordings from normal-hearing adults in an immersive multi-talker environment, we measured the neural encoding of two competing speech streams amid background babble. Participants were cued to switch attention between streams every 15–30 s. Neural tracking was assessed via Temporal Response Functions (TRF), confirming reliable decoding of attentional focus. Our results indicate asymmetric disengagement and engagement processes during attention switches, where the neural tracking of the new target stream emerges before...

item.title: ‘People remained in the LRA partly because they had wives and children’: forced families as a tool of warfare in the Lord’s Resistance Army
item.description:
Authors: Myriam Denov, Anaïs Cadieux Van Vliet, Rodney Buadi Nkrumah, Atim Angela Lakor

Source: Critical Military Studies

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canadian institution term detected

Abstract: The Lord’s Resistance Army (LRA) is well known for the violence it has waged across the African Great Lakes region. While the armed group has been studied extensively, limited attention has been paid to the deliberate creation of families formed through forced marriage and the birth of children conceived through sexual violence. In this article, we explore the strategic value and impact of the creation of LRA ‘families’ through the perspectives of 20 former male LRA members who were abducted into the LRA and who had fathered children through forced marriage. Drawing on interviews with former LRA members while building on the Militarized Social Reproduction framework we highlight the ways in which families were strategically created by LRA leadership to serve key functions. First, families helped to facilitate, control, and maximize labour production, ultimately contributing to the LRA’s...

item.title: The minor spliceosome component U4atac regulates JAK/STAT signaling to modulate hematopoiesis and immune responses in Drosophila melanogaster
item.description:
Authors: Dania Shikara, Eden Bishop, Nathan Barton, Stephanie Makdissi, Senthilkumar Kailasam, Attila György, Daria E. Siekhaus, Maria Carla Borroto et al.

Source: Nature Communications

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canadian institution term detected

Abstract: The small nuclear RNA U4atac is a core component of the minor spliceosome. In humans, homozygous or compound heterozygous point mutations in U4atac cause rare developmental disorders, such as Roifman syndrome, characterized by growth restriction, brain anomalies, and immune deficiency. To better define the pathophysiological role of U4atac mutations, we here establish a model of minor spliceosome dysfunction by generating a Drosophila melanogaster CRISPR/Cas9-induced U4atac mutant in the highly conserved stem II region. U4atac homozygous mutants exhibit growth and neurodevelopmental defects, immunodeficiency, and gastrointestinal symptoms. Using bulk RNA-sequencing and functional assays, we reveal that mutations in U4atac affect the splicing of a large set of transcripts involved in innate immunity, hematopoiesis, and intestinal cell functions, including the Drosophila Janus kinase...

item.title: Predictors of perceived social support and loneliness over time: A population-based study in Switzerland, 2021-2025
item.description:
Authors: Stephanie Schrempft, Hélène Baysson, Roxane Dumont, Silvia Stringhini, Idris Guessous, M. Nehme

Source: Public Health

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canadian institution term detected

Abstract: Objectives Identifying predictors of strong social connections is essential for the development of successful public health promotion strategies. However, longitudinal research on this topic is limited. As different measures of social connection may influence health via distinct pathways, we examined predictors of two measures – perceived social support and loneliness – over time. Study design Population-based longitudinal cohort study. Methods Data were from 6311 adults (mean age = 51 years [range 18–96 years], 58% women) assessed every year from 2021 to 2025. We used multivariate linear mixed-effects models to examine predictors of perceived social support and loneliness, with linear combinations of model coefficients (lincom) to test whether the effects differed between the two outcomes. Results Common predictors of (low) perceived support and (heightened) loneliness included living...

item.title: Omics research in Parkinson’s disease: evolution, integrated analysis, pathogenic mechanisms, biomarkers, and therapeutic targets
item.description:
Authors: Ruizhe Cao, Xue Bai, Huan Zhong

Source: Frontiers in Molecular Neuroscience

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canadian institution term detected

Abstract: Parkinson’s Disease (PD) is a common neurodegenerative disorder that has been widely investigated using omics approaches over 2020–2025. This review focuses on the transition from single-omics to integrative multi-omics analysis, which helps overcome the constraints of individual omics strategies and provides a more systematic understanding of PD pathogenesis. Key topics include the elucidation of core mechanisms (mitochondrial dysfunction, lipid metabolic disturbance, α -synucleinopathy, and gut–brain axis impairment), the development of biomarkers (shifting from invasive cerebrospinal fluid samples to less invasive plasma/urine panels with improved performance), and the identification of potential interventional targets (signaling pathways, key molecules, and gut microbiota). Technical advances include refined single-omics detection, AI-assisted multi-omics integration, and the...

item.title: Neurotechnology through the lens of users and as a novel field for society
item.description:
Authors: Danesh Ashouri, Ludwig Weh, Vera Borrmann, Christopher Coenen, Eva Maria Kraft, Viviana Loufs, Wenzel Mehnert, Paula Muhr et al.

Source: Frontiers in Neurology

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canadian institution term detected

Abstract: Successful and responsible innovation in neurotechnology requires clear ethical priorities and a deep understanding of individual and societal needs as well as public concerns. Recent cases of consumer exploitation, misleading claims, and inadequate patient aftercare reveal critical gaps in current practices and underscore the urgent need for more ethical, transparent, and user-centered engagement in this rapidly developing field. This study focuses on four complementary domains: (1) neuroethics and embodiment; (2) the cultural embedding of neurotechnologies; (3) art and culture in relation to neurotechnology; and (4) human enhancement, technovisions, and sociotechnical imaginaries. Across these domains, the manuscript explores user and societal perceptions, highlighting often overlooked asymmetries in communication between scientists and entrepreneurs and those who ultimately receive...

item.title: Student-centered leadership in higher education: Perspectives from academic Department Heads
item.description:
Authors: Yu Zan, Paul Newton

Source: Educational Management Administration & Leadership

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in abstract: canadian

Abstract: Student engagement, learning outcomes, and competency development have garnered growing attention in higher education. However, academic leaders often face challenges in fostering holistic student learning while also enhancing student career prospects. Drawing on student-centered leadership (SCL) theory, this study examines how university Department Heads (DHs) facilitate student learning and development, and explores the extent to which the K-12 SCL model is transferable to higher education contexts. The case study draws on semistructured interviews with eight DHs in a Canadian university. Data were analyzed using abductive reasoning. Findings show that DHs acted as operational actors who sought to improve learning outcomes by integrating high-impact practices into, and enhancing the quality of, teaching and curricula. A deep analysis of the findings suggests that DHs were required to...

item.title: Triticale and barley silage in backgrounding diets for beef steers: Effects on forage characteristics, steer performance, and system economics
item.description:
Authors: H.A. Lardner, B. Ross, D. Damiran, K.A. Larson, E. van Cleef, R.D. Oloo

Source: Applied Animal Science

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in abstract: canada

Abstract: ABSTRACT Objective This 3-yr study evaluated the suitability of 2 varieties (Taza and Bunker) of triticale silage versus barley silage in beef backgrounding systems. Materials and Methods The experiment assessed forage yield and quality, steer performance, feed sort-ing, and economic outcomes. Fall-weaned crossbred steer calves were used in the study, 402 in 2016 (308 ± 22 kg), 238 in 2017 (335 ± 23 kg), and 299 in 2018 (316 ± 18 kg). Animals were stratified by BW and randomly allo-cated to 1 of 3 isonitrogenous, isocaloric TMR diets (n = 4 replications per diet each year). Feedlot performance data were analyzed using a linear mixed model assuming a completely randomized design. Results and Discussion Across years, Bunker and Taza triticale had a greater forage yield (6,749 and 6,592 kg of DM/ha, respectively) than barley (6,008 kg of DM/ ha). Barley silage contained less ADF, NDF, and...

item.title: Social Media Engagement With Extreme Weather in Northern Climates: Natural Language Processing for Multi‐Directional Communication
item.description:
Authors: Renee Sieber, Andrei Mircea, Drew Bush, Frida Buitron, Zander Bamford‐Brown

Source: Journal of Contingencies and Crisis Management

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in abstract: canada

Abstract: ABSTRACT Meteorological information, conveyed through official alerts, is a daily presence in people's lives, aimed at enhancing preparedness and situational awareness. Such information can be found across multiple platforms, including television, radio, print publications and social media and is often mediated by third‐party broadcasters, social media personalities and others. Whereas the needs of forecasters and emergency managers in providing this information and managing weather events have been well‐studied in the literature; less attention has been paid to how the public engages with and acts upon such expert‐driven and real‐time weather information. Our study utilises Natural Language Processing (NLP) to examine public attention to the communication of disruptive weather events through Specific Area Message Encoding (SAME) and officially generated hashtag alerts during winter...

item.title: Fluorescence-based post-mortem interval estimation using cell lysate from DNA/RNA co-extraction of human soft tissue
item.description:
Authors: Colin Elliott, Aaron B. A. Shafer, Shari L. Forbes, Theresa Stotesbury

Source: ChemRxiv

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in abstract: canada, québec

Abstract: Post-mortem interval (PMI) estimation is an important element in medico-legal death investigations, as reliable estimates can establish timelines and generate investigative leads. Various analytical techniques are used to monitor biomolecular degradation throughout decomposition, with promising results obtained from fluorescence spectroscopy. In this work, we analyzed the fluorescent profile of cell lysates from 96 biopsy punches collected from 14 human cadavers at a human taphonomic facility in Québec, Canada, with PMIs ranging from 2 to 1098 days. Excitation-emission matrices (EEMs) were acquired for each lysate and deconvoluted using parallel factor analysis for fluorophore identification. We analyzed both composite and tissue-specific (muscle, adipose, and skin) samples. Overall, tryptophan fluorescence decreased with increasing PMI, whereas fluorescence between 400-500 nm,...

item.title: Bachelier's Theory of Speculation Revisited: A Reproducible Reconstruction of the Origins of Quantitative Finance
item.description:
Authors: Alpha Kabinet TOURE

Source: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in abstract: inuit

Abstract: Louis Bachelier’s 1900 doctoral thesis, Théorie de la Spéculation, is one of the earliest mathematical foundations of modern quantitative finance. Long before the standardization of stochastic calculus in financial economics, Bachelier introduced a probabilistic framework for market price fluctuations and derived option-pricing relations based on what is now recognized as arithmetic Brownian motion. This paper provides a modern, reproducible reconstruction of Bachelier’s theory. We reformulate the core model in contemporary notation, derive its martingale and variance-scaling properties, recover the Bachelier European call pricing formula, and validate the formula numerically through Monte Carlo simulation. We further compare the Bachelier model with Black–Scholes in a low-relative-volatility regime, emphasizing both the historical continuity and the structural differences between...

item.title: Physical activity in social contexts during the COVID-19 pandemic: a latent transition analysis
item.description:
Authors: Nerida Koert van der Linden, Matthew Bourke, Patricia Tucker, Jennifer D. Irwin, Shauna M. Burke

Source: BMC Public Health

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in abstract: canada, ontario

Abstract: While physical activity behaviours have been explored during the COVID-19 pandemic, little is known about the social contexts in which physical activity took place during this time of crisis. The purpose of this longitudinal study was to explore the physical activity-related social contexts in which young people (aged 15–24) engaged during the first two years of the COVID-19 pandemic in Ontario, Canada. Specific objectives included identifying: (1) how patterns of engagement in social contexts changed over time; and (2) demographic/descriptive predictors of social context-related class membership. Online surveys were completed from: (1) July–September 2020; (2) November 2020–January 2021; and (3) July 2021–January 2022. Data on gender, living situation, physical activity, and physical activity-related social contexts were collected. Latent transition analysis was conducted to determine...

item.title: Diversity of modern turbidity currents in a land-detached submarine canyon
item.description:
Authors: Alexandre Normandeau, Kimberley A. Jenner, C. Richards, Blair Greenan, Michael Z. Li, D. Calvin Campbell, Joan Eamer, Pierre‐Arnaud Desiage

Source: Geology

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in abstract: canada

Abstract: Turbidity currents in land-detached submarine canyons have long been considered largely inactive; however, recent observations offshore western Europe show that they can occur annually, challenging the highstand paradigm. Direct observations of turbidity currents in land-detached canyons remain rare, which has led to simplified assumptions about their behavior. Here, we combined autonomous underwater vehicle (AUV) surveys, mooring observations, and a box-core transect to examine the recent activity of turbidity currents in the land-detached Shortland Canyon, eastern Canada. Box cores comprise a turbidite with evidence of a dense basal layer, likely associated with rare large external triggers. AUV bathymetry does not support frequent turbidity currents with dense basal layers, as crescentic bed forms are poorly developed in the thalweg. AUV surveys and box cores indicate that most upper...

item.title: Interdisciplinary training in the gaps: exploring alumni perceptions and learning outcomes
item.description:
Authors: Marie-France Gévry, Pascale Ropars, Keith Lévesque

Source: Humanities and Social Sciences Communications

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in abstract: canada, quebec

Abstract: Abstract The increase in complex societal challenges requires collaboration between disciplines to generate new knowledge and develop effective solutions. Higher education institutions play a key role in fostering interdisciplinary (ID) research, but must innovate to adapt their educational systems to train the next generation of researchers. Understanding graduate students’ perceptions of ID training and their learning outcomes is also essential to ensuring the education provided effectively prepares them for ID practice. This study examines the Sentinel North Training Program (SNTP) at Université Laval (Quebec City, Canada), which offers training in the interstices of existing structures to build ID capacity among graduate students and postdoctoral fellows. The SNTP Conceptual Model and Competency Framework are presented, alongside findings from an exploratory evaluation of alumni...

item.title: A prospective cohort study of serum sickness-like reactions in the pediatric population
item.description:
Authors: Blanca R. Del Pozzo‐Magaña, Awatif M. Abuzgaia, Renee-Gabrielle Fajardo, Carmen Liy Wong, Stephanie Leung, Michael Rieder, Alejandro Lazo‐Langner

Source: Paediatrics & Child Health

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in abstract: ontario

Abstract: Abstract Background Serum sickness-like reaction (SSLR) is a hypersensitivity response, typically triggered by antibiotics or infections, presenting with rash, joint pain, and fever. Misdiagnosis is common due to the lack of standardized diagnostic criteria. Objective To characterize clinical and laboratory features of SSLR in pediatric patients to improve diagnostic accuracy and management. Methods A prospective cohort study was conducted at the Children's Hospital in London, Ontario, from October 2022 to September 2024. Children (0 to 18 years) presenting with rash and joint pain or edema were enrolled. Data included clinical presentation, laboratory findings, joint ultrasound results, and treatment outcomes. Results Of 27 patients, 59% were males. The mean age at the presentation was 4 years. Amoxicillin was the most frequent trigger (85.2%). Annular morphology was the most common...

item.title: Optimized Test Utilization Significantly Increased the Positive Detection Rate for Myeloproliferative Neoplasms
item.description:
Authors: Landry Nfonsam, Melody (Zhenchen) Qi, Darci T. Butcher, Clinton J.V. Campbell, Amanda Cocca, Shreyash Dalmia, Gwynivere A Davies, Betty-Ann Hohenadel et al.

Source: Cancers

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in abstract: canada, bc, hamilton

Abstract: Background: Molecular testing for myeloproliferative neoplasms (MPNs) without defined clinical criteria can lead to inefficient laboratory utilization without proportional diagnostic benefit. Methods: We evaluated the impact of implementing clinical acceptance criteria, including unexplained abnormal blood counts, unusual-site thrombosis, unexplained hepatosplenomegaly, and/or leukoerythroblastic blood film for gene (JAK2, CALR, MPL and/or BCR::ABL1) testing of patients with suspected MPNs in Hamilton, Canada and surrounding areas. The impact of testing criteria on ordering practices and diagnostic yields was assessed by retrospective examination of 3590 MPNs test requests submitted between 1 January and 31 December 2025; including 5.5 months of permissive testing (pre-implementation period), 2 months with a transition in testing criteria (grace period) and 4.5 months with restricted...

item.title: Evaluation of the Efficacy of Fungicide with Different Application Timings for Mitigation of Fusarium Head Blight in Winter Rye
item.description:
Authors: Duoduo Wang, Jamie Larsen, María Antonia Henríquez, Anita Brule-Babel

Source: Plant Disease

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in abstract: canada, winnipeg

Abstract: Fusarium head blight (FHB) is one of the most important diseases of cereals. Several foliar fungicides have been used to manage FHB in North America. Fungicide trials were conducted in Winnipeg and Carman from 2018 to 2020 to evaluate the efficacy of different application times of the fungicide prothioconazole + tebuconazole for the mitigation of FHB incidence, severity, index, Fusarium-damaged kernels (FDK), and deoxynivalenol (DON) in winter rye. Treatments consisted of an inoculated-untreated check (IUC), an uninoculated-untreated check (UUC), and fungicide applications at heading, 10% anthesis (10A), 80% anthesis (80A), and 6 days after 50% anthesis (6DA50A). Fungicide application at 10A resulted in the highest efficacy for reducing FHB incidence, severity, index, FDK, and DON content in winter rye. Fungicide applications at 10A and 80A increased yield compared with the IUC....

item.title: Efficacy and safety of green-lipped mussel powder supplementation in adults with knee osteoarthritis: a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial
item.description:
Authors: Hyun-Tae Kim, Bong-Jin Shin, Bong-Jin Shin, Yeon-Woo Lee, Hye-Jin Park, Sun‐Young Park, Eui-Hyoung Hwang, Man‐Suk Hwang et al.

Source: BMC Complementary Medicine and Therapies

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in abstract: ontario

Abstract: Knee osteoarthritis (OA) is a prevalent degenerative joint disease associated with pain, functional limitation, and reduced quality of life. Although nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs are widely prescribed, long-term use is limited by safety concerns. Green-lipped mussel powder (GLMP) has demonstrated anti-inflammatory and chondroprotective effects in preclinical studies. This trial evaluated the efficacy and safety of GLMP supplementation in adults with knee OA. Adults aged 40–75 years with symptomatic radiographic knee OA were randomly assigned (1:1) to receive GLMP (1,000 mg/day) or placebo for 12 weeks. Assessments occurred at baseline, week 6, and week 12. The primary endpoint was change in the Western Ontario and McMaster Universities Osteoarthritis Index (WOMAC) total score. Secondary endpoints were WOMAC subscales (pain, stiffness, physical function), visual analog scale...

item.title: Introducing family medicine in Tanzania: strengthening primary health care through the 5 Cs—advocacy through a deliberative dialogue symposium-a case study of multinational academic collaboration
item.description:
Authors: Donatus Rutajama Mutasingwa, Nancy Matillya, Enica Richard, Matilda Alfred Mkonyi, Elichilia R. Shao, Michael Burke, H. Ziegler, Esther M. Johnston et al.

Source: Frontiers in Medicine

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in abstract: inuit

Abstract: Tanzania is currently advancing universal health coverage (UHC) through the 2026 launch of its Universal Health Insurance scheme, creating a critical policy window to strengthen primary health care (PHC). This paper reflects on the first Family Medicine Symposium in Morogoro (January 2026), a milestone event designed to advocate for the integration of Family Medicine (FM) into the national health system. Utilizing a deliberative dialogue approach, the symposium convened 51 diverse stakeholders—including Tanzanian policymakers and academic partners from eight countries—to examine FM through the “5 Cs” framework: first-contact access, continuity, comprehensiveness, coordination, and person-centered care. The event featured expert presentations, case vignettes, and facilitated discussions structured around the 5 Cs. In addition to the 5 Cs we also discussed training, financing, and...

item.title: Ancient sea turtle (Chelonioidea) ZooMS identifications illuminate species-specific human interactions and long-term populations changes for Curaçao and Bonaire
item.description:
Authors: Christine Conlan, Claudia T. Kraan, Lindsey Paskulin, Jay Hilsden, Ruoxin Yuan, Pengpeng Chen, Camilla Speller, Christina M. Giovas

Source: Journal of Archaeological Science Reports

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in abstract: bc

Abstract: Caribbean sea turtle histories are deeply intertwined with past human societies, with zooarchaeological evidence suggesting their dietary and socio-cultural importance from as early as 3370 BCE. However, the high fragmentation typical of archaeological sea turtle bones has generally limited morphological identifications to higher taxonomic levels, hindering species-specific assessments of interaction with humans and their legacy effects. We applied ZooMS to 87 highly fragmented sea turtle specimens from seven archaeological sites in the arid, tropical Caribbean islands of Curaçao and Bonaire, spanning 2310 BCE to the 19th century, to reconstruct past sea turtle species richness, relative abundance, and human influences on sea turtle populations. Our results provide long-term evidence for human interactions with green ( Chelonia mydas ), hawksbill ( Eretmochelys imbricata ), and...

item.title: SwissPhenoCam: A country-scale dataset of tree-level phenocam greenness captures species-specific phenological variation along elevation gradients in Switzerland
item.description:
Authors: Vivien Sainte Fare Garnot, J. Jelle Lever, Maaike de Boer, Lynsay Spafford, Yann Vitasse, Christian Sigg, Barbara Pietragalla, Roman Zweifel et al.

Source: Unknown source

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in abstract: bc

Abstract: Abstract. Vegetation phenology, the seasonal timing of recurring plant life-cycle events, is a key regulator of ecosystem functioning, carbon and water cycling, and species interactions, while also serving as a sensitive indicator of climate variability and climate change. However, existing phenological observations often face trade-offs between species-level detail, temporal frequency, and spatial coverage. Networks of near-surface digital repeat cameras (phenocams) deliver robust, high-frequency phenological signals, but their spatial coverage remains a limitation. In addition to expanding such phenocam networks, this limitation can be tackled by repurposing the outdoor cameras already deployed for other purposes. Here, we present SwissPhenoCam, a country-scale dataset of tree-level phenological greenness observations derived from a network of outdoor digital cameras distributed...

item.title: SMEs performance in public procurement and the Italian Legality Rating
item.description:
Authors: Andrea Fazio, Erminia Florio, Gustavo Piga

Source: Small Business Economics

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in abstract: inuit

Abstract: Abstract This study investigates the impact of the Italian Legality Rating (LR) on small and medium enterprises (SMEs) in public procurement. The LR, issued by the Italian Competition Authority, evaluates firms based on fiscal transparency, anti-corruption practices, corporate responsibility, and sustainability. While larger firms are more likely to obtain higher LR scores, SMEs benefit significantly from holding the certification. Using firm-level data and a Regression Discontinuity Design, we find that the LR doubles SMEs’ tender-winning probabilities, highlighting its role as a strong institutional signal. Our findings emphasize the LR’s potential to enhance SMEs’ competitiveness in public procurement.

item.title: Bachelier's Theory of Speculation Revisited: A Reproducible Reconstruction of the Origins of Quantitative Finance
item.description:
Authors: Alpha Kabinet TOURE

Source: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)

Abstract: Louis Bachelier’s 1900 doctoral thesis, Théorie de la Spéculation, is one of the earliest mathematical foundations of modern quantitative finance. Long before the standardization of stochastic calculus in financial economics, Bachelier introduced a probabilistic framework for market price fluctuations and derived option-pricing relations based on what is now recognized as arithmetic Brownian motion. item.title: Survival estimates and their predictors in genetic frontotemporal dementia: an international, retrospective, cohort study
item.description:
Authors: Arabella Bouzigues, Mario Grassi, Valentina Cantoni, Enrico Premi, Sonia Bellini, Giuliano Binetti, Giancarlo Logroscino, Lucy L Russell et al.

Source: The Lancet Neurology

Abstract: Abstract. Our study domain encompasses the Athabasca Oil Sands Region, an industrial area where base cation-bearing fugitive dust from open-pit mining and forest fires coexists with elevated SO2 emissions from large stacks. Our pH-modulated dry deposition scheme links thin aqueous films on foliage to local chemical conditions, including alkaline dust accumulation. Abstract: Abstract Cellular processes are controlled by interconnected networks of protein–protein interactions that can be dynamically regulated by post-translational modifications such as phosphorylation. Mass spectrometry (MS)-based approaches have emerged as key technologies to study both protein function and their dynamic regulation at a network level. Modern proteomics allows investigators to study how signaling networks are rewired in response to genetic lesions, external cues, and targeted therapies, enabling the comparison of baseline (steady-state) networks to perturbed states. Abstract: This article compares and contrasts two approaches to university inclusion in Quebec: individual accommodations and inclusive pedagogy. The universal approach transforms learning environments to anticipate the diversity of needs. It relies on flexible practices, a diversification of support materials and assessments, and is situated within a systemic perspective. The article describes the challenges of implementing inclusive pedagogy: varying levels of understanding, dependence on individual will, organizational constraints, and tensions between flexibility and academic standards. Abstract: Abstract. The new mckelveyite group mineral bainbridgeite-(NdCe), ideally Na2Ba2NdCe(CO3)6⚫3H2O, was found at Mont Saint-Hilaire, Quebec, Canada. It occurs in two distinct environments: (1) in a “carbonate pegmatite” as pale-yellow or orange barrel-shaped short prismatic crystals up to 2 mm in size, where bainbridgeite-(NdCe) forms thin rims while the core is composed of bainbridgeite-(YCe), and (2) in a hornfels rock as white or pale-grey tabular saucer-shaped crystals up to 0.5 mm in size. It has a white streak, a vitreous lustre, and no cleavage. Dcalc is 3.49 g cm−3. Bainbridgeite-(NdCe) is optically biaxial (+), α=1.577(3), β=1.592(3), γ=1.657(3), 2V (meas.) = 40(3)°, and 2V (calc.) = 52° (589 nm). The IR spectrum is reported. Abstract: Background: Powassan virus is a cause of viral encephalitis transmitted by Ixodes ticks. A few weeks following a tick bite, patients with Powassan virus infection present with fever, encephalitis, disorientation, or aseptic meningitis. 10% of cases are fatal, and 50% of survivors experience long-term neurological sequelae. The diagnosis of Powassan virus is primarily based on serology; however, significant cross-reactivity exists with other flaviviruses. Testing is primarily by serology. Powassan virus polymerase chain reaction can be performed on cerebrospinal fluid, but the virus is rarely detectable by the time patients present with symptoms. Methods: This was a retrospective chart review of a single case of a woman with Powassan virus infection presenting with ascending paralysis. The relevant format here is the critical edition, a version of the literary text that is enhanced by a critical apparatus that can include collation (annotations of textual variation between versions of a text), explanatory notes, glosses, indices, and a critical introduction. Abstract: Abstract Introduction The urban environment increases the risk of inflammatory bowel disease (IBD). Specific environmental exposures involved in IBD etiology remain unknown. We examined the association between outdoor artificial light at night (ALAN) and IBD incidence, surgery, and health services utilization (HSU). Methods Using population-based deterministically linked health administrative data from Ontario, Canada we conducted a birth cohort study (incidence), matched case-control study (incidence), and cohort study (surgery, HSU). Individuals with IBD were identified using previously validated algorithms. ALAN, the average digital number of lights consistently present, was a 3-level variable: <35 (reference), 35-60, > 60. We used Cox proportional hazards models (birth cohort, surgery), conditional logistic regression (matched case-control study), and Poisson regression (HSU)....

item.title: Twine and Inky: A Hypertext Multi-tool for the Creative Writing Classroom
item.description:
Authors: Jeremy Andriano

Source: Journal of International Crisis and Risk Communication Research

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Twine (twinery.org) and ink/Inky (inklestudios.com/ink) are both digital authoring platforms for creating and sharing hypertext games and stories. Each platform employs easy to grasp markup languages for interconnecting hyperlinks and structuring text in ways that can be hidden from the reader, empowering the author to use hyperlinks for rhetorical and aesthetic purposes beyond simple ‘next-page’ linking. Additionally, each platform includes a robust suite of tutorial materials and built-in functionality for implementing advanced programming logics and for stylizing the text’s appearance on the screen, and each platform publishes as easily sharable .html files that can be read using any web browser. Despite these similarities, Twine and ink/Inky differ in significant ways that impact the style of the work and the author’s writing experience. More importantly, these differences are often...

item.title: Small Changes, Large Effects: A Chaos Theory Framework for Electronic Literature in Climate Education for Indian Primary Students
item.description:
Authors: Mazumdar, Mumtaz, Dr

Source: Journal of International Crisis and Risk Communication Research

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Climate change exemplifies chaos theory's core principle: small initial changes producing large, unpredictable outcomes—the butterfly effect. Similarly, climate education for young children operates as a complex system where small pedagogical interventions can cascade into transformative understanding and lifelong behavioral patterns. This paper applies chaos theory as a theoretical framework for understanding how electronic literature (e-lit) functions as one component within climate education for Indian primary students (Classes 1-5, ages 6-11). Drawing on Edward Lorenz's foundational work on sensitive dependence on initial conditions and applying chaos theory principles to educational contexts, this study examines how e-lit's affordances— interactivity, multimodality, and non-linearity—align with both climate systems' chaotic nature and children's non-linear learning processes....

item.title: Suicidal Ideation, Screen Time, and Online Harassment Among College Students: Gender Differences in a National Survey, United States, 2023-2024.
item.description:
Authors: Seungbin Oh, Ryemi Do, Soyeon Kim, Sasha Zhou

Source: PubMed

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: . 2026;116(8):1104-1115. https://doi.org/10.2105/AJPH.2026.308576).

item.title: Reach of Palliative Care in Parkinson Disease: Progress and Gaps After a National Team-Based Implementation Project.
item.description:
Authors: S. Seshadri, Umer Akbar, Peggy Auinger, Nicole Andrea Lessard, Megan Dini, Sally A. Norton, Hillary D. Lum, Jodi Summers Holtrop et al.

Source: PubMed

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: BACKGROUND AND OBJECTIVES: The value of palliative care (PC) is increasingly recognized in the care of people with Parkinson disease (PWP) and their carepartners. While clinical trials demonstrate PC improves quality of life for PWP and carepartners, little is known about the impact of PC on their experiences of receiving care in real-world settings. We designed and implemented a national outpatient PC project at the Parkinson's Foundation US-based Centers of Excellence (COEs) between August 2021 and November 2023. We aimed to assess preimplementation vs postimplementation changes in perceptions and experiences of PWP and carepartners related to the receipt of PC. METHODS: Between September and October 2023, an electronic survey was distributed through the Parkinson's Foundation to PWP and carepartners who received care at US-based COEs and non-COEs. Survey questions assessed knowledge,...

item.title: Jacques Lecoq’s Embodied and Ecological Actor Training
item.description:
Authors: Evans, Mark; id_orcid 0000-0003-0564-0771, Pearce, Michael, Kenney, Denise

Source: Pure (Coventry University)

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in abstract: canada

Abstract: This chapter argues that Jacques Lecoq’s pedagogy, which nurtures creative expression through embodied identification with the more-than-human world and operates through non-hierarchical group creation, can be understood as a form of ecological enquiry, one that functions less as a fixed technique and more as an epistemology: a living methodology. <br/> <br/>Jacques Lecoq founded his school in Paris in 1956. Over the last seventy years, it has trained over 5,000 graduates from more than 80 countries, many of whom have gone on to teach and adapt his approach across the globe. Lecoq’s pedagogy offers students a distinctive journey of creative self-discovery. Unlike mainstream theatre programmes that centre on human psychological and social relations, Lecoq’s approach is predicated on the maxim tout bouge (‘everything moves’), a philosophy that invites students to experience their human...

item.title: When Identification Drives Giving: Toward Relationally Aware Governance Architectures for Nonprofit Information Systems
item.description:
Authors: Anca Veronica Baban

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Nonprofit CRM architectures should be governed as relational stewardship systems rather than transactional management tools, because long-term donor commitment depends more on identification and trust than on observable giving behavior alone. Current CRM designs privilege measurable indicators such as gift frequency, conversion rates, and donor scoring, while rendering shared identity, symbolic affiliation, and evolving trust largely invisible in organizational decision-making. This creates a governance mismatch in which the relationships fundraisers rely on most are poorly represented within the systems intended to support them. Emerging predictive AI intensifies this tension by optimizing behavioral proxies while further obscuring the relational conditions that sustain philanthropic commitment. The paper argues that CRM schemas encode governance assumptions about which donor...

item.title: Beyond AI as a Tool or Teammate: Information Quality as a Driver of Human–AI Collaboration and AI Use Intentions
item.description:
Authors: Aydin Farrokhi, Noel J. Thomas

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) is increasingly embedded within organizational processes, transforming how employees and users interact with technology in hybrid human–AI environments. Prior research shows that AI increasingly augments rather than replaces human capabilities across service, leadership, teamwork, learning, and decision-making settings (Gnewuch et al., 2024). Studies further demonstrate that perceptions of AI significantly influence collaboration, engagement, and adoption outcomes (Seeber et al., 2020). Existing literature has largely focused on AI role framing, such as whether AI is perceived as a tool or teammate, alongside related concepts including social presence, trust, and anthropomorphism. Despite these advances, limited attention has been given to the role of perceived information quality in strengthening collaborative human–AI systems. This gap is important because...

item.title: Classification-Induced Data Bias: How Class-Based Schemas Distort Data at Collection
item.description:
Authors: Aida Nouri, Jeffrey Parsons

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Data bias is a recognized problem in research and data-driven decision-making. Existing treatments focus on how data was collected: sampling bias from non-representative selection, measurement bias from recording errors, annotation bias from inconsistent labeling, and omitted variable bias from excluded variables (Mehrabi et al., 2021). These accounts share a common assumption: the data collection schema is neutral. The schema, meaning the set of categories that structure what gets recorded, is treated as a given rather than a source of bias. This paper challenges that assumption and introduces classification-induced data bias: systematic distortion arising from the imposition of a class-based schema at the point of data collection. Class-based schemas, which require contributors to assign every observation to a predefined category, are the foundational assumption of structured data...

item.title: Bridges Not Walls: Building Research Networks in the Americas
item.description:
Authors: Kevin Scheibe, Alexandre Reis Graeml, Elaine Mosconi, Jose Pineda, Deanna House, Yang Lee, Van Slyke, Craig

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: International research collaboration is essential to advancing Information Systems (IS) scholarship and addressing complex societal, organizational, and technological challenges. Within AIS Region 1, there is significant opportunity to strengthen cross-border and cross-chapter research networks that leverage the region’s linguistic, cultural, institutional, and methodological diversity. At the same time, researchers face persistent barriers, including differences in funding systems, institutional incentives, language, infrastructure, mobility, and access to collaborative networks. Facilitated by VP Region 1, this panel brings together representatives from the six AIS Region 1 chapters—BRAIS, CANAIS, LACAIS, NEAIS, MWAIS, and SAIS—to discuss challenges and opportunities in building stronger research connections across the Americas. The panel will examine how AIS chapters can foster...

item.title: Between Expertise and Vulnerability: The Visual Construction of IT Security Roles in Cybersecurity Governance
item.description:
Authors: Guy Alain Sylvanus Kouyo, Rohit Nishant, Hager Khechine

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Cybersecurity governance involves persistent misalignments between operational accountability and strategic authority, yet how these roles are symbolically constructed through visual discourse remains unexplored. This study examines how cybersecurity editorial cartoons function as sensegiving artifacts shaping organizational perceptions of information technology (IT) security teams. Drawing on sensemaking theory and IT governance research, we apply Panofsky's iconological framework to analyze twelve cartoons published by Cybersecurity Ventures between 2021 and 2025. The study aims to extend sensemaking theory into the visual domain, illuminate governance asymmetry, and demonstrate the value of iconological analysis for IS research.

item.title: Meta-Governance of Autonomous AI Agents: A Policy-as-Code Architecture for Real-Time GRC in Multi-Agent Systems
item.description:
Authors: Himanshu Joshi, Shivani Shukla, Sunita Kumari, Manas Joshi

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Enterprises deploying autonomous multi-agent AI systems face a fundamental governance gap:- traditional IT Governance, Risk, and Compliance (GRC) frameworks operate at human speed while agentic AI systems operate at machine speed, rendering conventional oversight architecturally incompatible with emerging threats. This paper introduces meta-governance as a novel IS (Information Systems) security construct: the use of AI governance agents to autonomously monitor, evaluate, and intervene in the behavior of operational AI agent fleets. We present MOM-GS-MAS (Monitoring, Observability, and Management Module for Governance and Security of Multi-Agent Systems), a production-ready meta-governance platform deploying 16 specialized governance agents across four Safety, Alignment, Governance, and Security (SAGS) pillars. Grounded in Design Science Research (DSR), we evaluate MOM-GS-MAS through...

item.title: IS Privacy Research: Problematizing and Theorizing Theory through Models
item.description:
Authors: William Bonner

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: The IS field is being challenged to take theory and theorizing more seriously. This paper engages in theorizing by problematizing the notion of models as theory. The Antecedents-Privacy Concern-Outcome model (APCO), of IS privacy research, is used as an exemplar to articulate limitations of models as theory. These limitations stem in part from foundational assumptions made in the IS field’s origins impacting questions asked and whose questions they are, as well as embedded but unacknowledged theories of management, within the model. Models, created from propositions, are confounded by what is outside the model; outside entirely, or manifesting itself in the model different from their manifestation in practice, outside the model. This has a negative impact on relevance. This is contrasted with the Privacy in Context framework where models are developed from the framework and there is no...

item.title: Transparent Digital Green Nudging for Sustainable Choices on eTourism Platforms
item.description:
Authors: Dominique Welt, Éline Jongmans, Alexander Chung

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Most travel bookings now occur through online platforms. Given the current urgency of global climate change, there is a need for such platforms to promote sustainable options. While certain platforms have adopted digital green nudging techniques to encourage sustainable choices, there are increasing calls to ensure that such design approaches ethically respect consumers’ freedom of choice and preferences. To this end, this study examines the practice of transparent digital green nudging. We further examine whether the effectiveness of this approach depends on the platform’s profit model. We test our hypotheses using a web-based experiment with 293 participants. Our results show that nudges are effective in increasing consumers’ propensity to make sustainable choices, that their effectiveness is not diminished when nudges are transparent, and that these effects hold for both for-profit...

item.title: When Resilience Becomes Risk: How Cybersecurity Incidents Reshape Supply Chain Recovery Strategies
item.description:
Authors: Xuecong Lu, Junyi Yang, Chunming Shi, Jie Mi, Si Liu

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Digital supply chains rely on tightly integrated information systems that improve coordination and resilience but also increase cybersecurity vulnerability. This study examines how cybersecurity incidents affect operational recovery performance and how these effects depend on firms’ pre-incident recovery structure and cybersecurity governance. We define Cyber-Structural Misalignment as a condition in which digital connectivity exceeds governance capacity to manage cyber risk. We argue that cyber incidents weaken operational recovery, that redundancy and digital integration can amplify this effect, and that strong cybersecurity governance mitigates disruption and supports recovery.

item.title: Governance Readiness Gaps in Organizational AI Deployment: A Triangulated Analysis of Threats, Incidents, and Practice
item.description:
Authors: Carlos Denner dos Santos, Elaine Mosconi

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) has shifted from boardroom aspiration to organizational imperative, yet governance readiness lags deployment pace. Drawing on dynamic capabilities and institutional decoupling, we investigate how trust readiness (governance capability) and integration readiness (implementation capability) shape AI governance outcomes. Triangulating MITRE ATLAS, the AI Incident Database, and the U.S. Federal AI Use Case Inventory (1,757 deployments), our analysis reveals governance theater: a majority of AI deployments report internal review approval, yet only a small fraction report substantive safeguards. Risk-tiering does not rescue governance depth. Splitting trust readiness uncovers a suppression effect: surface approvals facilitate deployment while substantive safeguards dampen it. Integration readiness remains the dominant predictor of operational deployment....

item.title: Supporting Scalable e-Participation through LLM-Assisted Proposal Writing and Summarisation
item.description:
Authors: Miguel Domingues, Marco Neto, Carla Guerra, Emanuel Pacheco, Luis Cordeiro

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: This paper proposes an LLM-assisted framework scaling citizen engagement while preserving democratic legitimacy. Unlike existing platforms focused on clustering, it provides drafting support and summarisation via human-in-the-loop architecture with modular microservices and guardrails. A proof-of-concept on real participatory budgeting data shows LLMs transform informal inputs into compliant proposals and actionable summaries (nvidia-qwen3-80B: FS=0.92, zero hallucinations). Results position LLMs as accountable civic infrastructure enhancing e-inclusion and reducing administrative burden, a key for e-democracy scalability.

item.title: Eclipsing Expertise: Linguistic Eloquence as a Catalyst for Cognitive Passivity in Generative AI Interactions
item.description:
Authors: Khadija Essaied, Sehl Mellouli, Richard Khoury

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Generative AI systems increasingly support expert decision making, yet their highly fluent and polished explanations may unintentionally weaken professional vigilance. This study examines how linguistic eloquence in generative AI outputs influences experts’ ability to detect reasoning errors in high stakes contexts. Using a between-subjects experiment, we manipulate the fluency of an AI-based decision support system while keeping its analytical content constant, including a predefined logical error. Drawing on Dual Process Theory and Cognitive Fit Theory, this study investigates whether stylistic fluency triggers heuristic processing, reduces cognitive effort, and encourages premature cognitive offloading. Expected contributions include extending existing cognitive theories by introducing linguistic cognitive fit, showing how stylistic alignment between AI language and expert...

item.title: Task AI Fit – Misplaced Trust, Miscalibrated Reliance and Decision Vulnerability
item.description:
Authors: Cem Ozer, Ozgur Turetken

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) has become an omnipresent support technology for a variety of decisions. Yet people's beliefs about how well AI suits a task, their perceived task-AI fit, may diverge from actual task-AI fit. This divergence creates miscalibrated reliance on AI. When perceived fit is too high, users over-rely on AI, which can lead to inferior outcomes. When perceived fit is too low, users dismiss useful AI guidance missing out on better decision opportunities. Both of these outcomes represent decision vulnerabilities. Drawing on task-technology fit (TTF) and trust in automation theories, we propose a model with two antecedents of AI reliance mediated by trust in AI: perceived AI capability and perceived self-capability. Reliance on AI is hypothesized to be associated with decision vulnerability (deviation from optimal decisions) moderated by the level of actual task-AI fit....

item.title: Cybersecurity Training via AI: Examining Employee Perceptions, Trust, and Readiness for AI-Enhanced Learning
item.description:
Authors: Massi Ahmed, Xingwei Yang, Wendi Zhou, Mehdi Kargar, Morteza Zihayat

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Phishing remains a major cybersecurity threat, yet existing training often fails to drive behavioral change. This study examines employees’ experiences with current cybersecurity training and their attitudes toward AI-based solutions. Drawing on the Unified Theory of Acceptance and Use of Technology (UTAUT) and Protection Motivation Theory (PMT), we design a survey to investigate these factors. Using data from 192 employees, results show that while current training is informative, it lacks engagement and effectiveness. Employees express a strong interest in AI-based training; however, adoption is constrained by privacy concerns and limited organizational support, highlighting key tensions between perceived benefits and risks.

item.title: Fitting In and Standing Out: Strategic CSR Positioning on Social Media and Financial Performance
item.description:
Authors: Yanda Tao, Changseung Yoo, Animesh Animesh, Kunsoo Han

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Corporate social responsibility (CSR) communication has shifted from periodic disclosure to continuous expression on social media. While prior research emphasizes CSR disclosure and its financial consequences, little is known about firms’ relative positioning in social media environments. This study examines whether differentiated CSR communication on social media influences financial performance. Drawing on optimal distinctiveness theory, we explore how firms’ assimilation and differentiation in CSR communication may influence financial performance. Using social media and financial data from S&P 500 firms between 2014 and 2019, we found that assimilation, reflected in more frequent CSR communication intensity, and differentiation, reflected in greater semantic distinctiveness, are both positively associated with financial performance. By conceptualizing social media CSR as a relative...

item.title: How Can Information Technology Use Deliver Triple Bottom Line Value?
item.description:
Authors: Philippe Marchildon, Pierre Hadaya, Jean-Mathieu Rose

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: This paper examines the contribution of information technology (IT) use to an organization’s triple bottom line (TBL). While prior research characterizes IT as simultaneously contributing to sustainability problems and solutions, limited empirical work explains why and how IT use generates sustainable organizational outcomes in practice. Building on a TBL understanding of sustainability and the IT benefits management literature, we propose to conduct an interpretive multiple-case study to uncover the mechanisms and conditions through which IT use enables economic, environmental, and social organizational outcomes. Four cases will be selected using a 2x2 matrix to control for technical and contextual variations. Data collection and data analysis will be performed iteratively with the aim of establishing the chain of causality that links IT use to sustainable impacts. Pilot findings...

item.title: Prevention or Recovery? Untangling the Effects of Top Management Team Governance on Cybersecurity Incidents
item.description:
Authors: Xuecong Lu, Prinkle Sharma, Haoze Chen, Junyi Yang

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: This study examines the impact of Top Management Team (TMT) involvement on cybersecurity performance, distinguishing between two critical outcomes: prevention (incident occurrence) and recovery (restoration time). Grounded in Upper Echelons Theory and the attention-based view, we deconstruct TMT involvement into two dimensions: breadth (cross-functional participation) and depth (intensity of engagement). Using a pooled cross-sectional dataset of 8,352 firm-year observations from the UK Cyber Security Breaches Survey (2018–2021), we propose that TMT breadth primarily enhances preventive vigilance, while TMT depth is more effective at accelerating post-incident recovery. Furthermore, we investigate how external stakeholder feedback moderates these relationships by amplifying executive accountability and information quality. By bridging the gap between ex ante governance and ex post...

item.title: The Emergence and Evolution of Psychological Ownership of IT
item.description:
Authors: Benedict Lewis Abotsi

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Psychological ownership of IT (POIT) has been studied as a static antecedent. This paper proposes that POIT evolves across three stages: initial adoption, continued use, and habitual use. We develop a longitudinal model where POIT transitions from an antecedent of use to a consequence of routinized use. We provide falsifiable criteria for testing causal inversion, specify boundary conditions, and identify cognitive offloading as the underlying mechanism. A three-wave design (T1=2–4 weeks, T2=8 weeks, T3=24 weeks) using cross-lagged panel modeling is proposed. Contributions include a dynamic theory of POIT and practical guidance for project managers.

item.title: When Outsourcing Meets AI: How Externalizing IT Functions Shapes Firms’ AI Readiness under Data-Sovereignty Shocks
item.description:
Authors: Benedict Lewis Abotsi, Paul Yuke Wang

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: This study examines how firms’ dependence on external IT providers affects their ability to adopt and adapt AI technologies under data sovereignty regulatory shocks. We argue that high dependence on outsourced IT infrastructure from dominant “hyperscaler” vendors erodes a firm’s internal reconfiguration capabilities, widening the “sovereignty-adaptability gap”. We theorize that firm size moderates this relationship, with smaller firms experiencing stronger negative effects. The study contributes to literature by identifying institutional shocks as distinct triggers for reconfiguration and by shifting focus from cost-efficiency to adaptability under regulatory uncertainty.

item.title: Governing Demographic Bias Across AI Retrieval Systems
item.description:
Authors: Archan Dutta, Vyanktesh Kanungo

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) retrieval systems increasingly support high-stakes decision-making in domains such as law enforcement, healthcare, and finance. Governance frameworks often view bias as a system-level metric. However, AI retrieval systems operate as staged infrastructures, where stages jointly shape the outcomes. This study examines how demographic bias manifests across three technical stages of AI retrieval systems: representation, retrieval, and decision ranking. Using an empirical study in a law enforcement context, we measure racial bias at each stage and demonstrate that bias metrics vary depending on where in the pipeline they are assessed. We argue that system-level auditing alone is insufficient for governance of AI-driven decision systems in high-stakes domains. Instead, we propose stage-aligned governance mechanisms for each stage of the AI retrieval system. Our...

item.title: Who Should Talk About Mental Health on Instagram? Credibility Signals and Engagement Dynamics
item.description:
Authors: Honeyeh Yazdizadeh, Samira Farivar

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Mental health advocacy is becoming widespread and influential, especially in the era of excessive use of social media platforms. Although many studies examine the involved issues, few take a multidimensional view linking the expertise–authenticity tension with engagement metrics. This paper examines a central tension in Instagram mental health advocacy: credibility versus relatability and how this trade-off shapes user engagement. While prior research has studied influencer credibility, branded content, and mental health messaging, it has rarely systematically compared licensed vs. unlicensed advocates alongside branded vs. non-branded framing using large-scale, post-level evidence. To address this gap, we analyze posts from 41 verified Instagram accounts (May 2025–May 2026). Using Natural Language Processing (NLP) and Top2Vec topic modeling, we identify dominant mental health themes...

item.title: Evaluative Misalignment in LLM-Supported Policy Analysis: Two Heuristic Cases
item.description:
Authors: Shengnan Yang, Xiaohua Awa Zhu

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used in policy analysis, where evaluation often relies on prompt optimization followed by output assessment. This ERF paper examines evaluative misalignment in two heuristic cases from our applied LLM work in policy analysis: qualitative coding and policy summarization. We identify two potential forms of misalignment in LLM evaluation processes: between human reasoning and model behavior, and among human evaluators. The cases show that plausible or high-scoring outputs may still diverge from the reasoning and contextual interpretation required in policy tasks. We suggest that LLM output evaluation in such settings should be understood as a process-centered, role-aligned activity. The paper offers a preliminary account of how evaluative misalignment emerges when output scores, model behavior, and human judgment are positioned within policy...

item.title: Stock Reactions to Cybersecurity Product Introductions: The Critical Roles of AI-Driven Innovation and R&D Intensity
item.description:
Authors: Haoze Chen, Xuecong Lu, Wei, Yuyan, Dr., Si Liu

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: This study examines stock market responses to cybersecurity product launch announcements by publicly traded U.S. firms. Using a preliminary sample of 14 announcements identified from news reports and press releases between 2021 and 2024, we estimate abnormal returns with the Fama–French–Carhart model. Average short-window reactions are modestly negative, suggesting that launch announcements do not uniformly create shareholder value in the immediate term. However, reactions vary across announcements: AI-driven launches and launches by firms with higher R&D intensity generate more favorable short-window returns. These findings suggest that investors respond more favorably when launch announcements signal stronger technological novelty and innovation credibility. The study extends research on the market consequences of proactive cybersecurity actions and identifies conditions under which...

item.title: Building an AI Literacy Curriculum: Insights from a Longitudinal Study
item.description:
Authors: Dominique Welt, Philippe Marchildon

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: As educational institutions redesign curricula to address the growing importance of artificial intelligence (AI), questions remain regarding both the content and sequencing of AI literacy instruction. This study proposes a longitudinal field experiment to examine whether the order in which technical and critical AI competencies are taught influences undergraduate business students’ AI literacy development and AI readiness. Drawing on UNESCO’s AI Competency Framework for Students, this experiment follows a pretest-posttest control group design comparing two instructional sequences: (1) technical followed by critical competencies, and (2) critical followed by technical competencies. Participants will be students enrolled in a multi-sections AI course and the intervention will be integrated into the course curriculum. Differences in gains in perceived and performative AI literacy, as well...

item.title: Os Impactos das Tecnologias Digitais no Acesso à Justiça do Trabalho para Grupos Socialmente Vulneráveis
item.description:
Authors: Gabriel Favalli Branco, Marlei Pozzebon

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: In Brazil, vulnerable populations face structural barriers to constitutional rights, positioning Labor Courts as essential guarantors. While Information and Communication Technologies (ICTs) aim to modernize the Judiciary, digital exclusion and infrastructure gaps disproportionately affect these groups, leaving the social impact of digitalization underexplored. This study addresses this gap by investigating how ICTs influence access to labor justice through an integrated theoretical lens: Capabilities Theory, Social Inclusion Theory, and the Access to Justice Movement. Adopting a qualitative approach, the research employs semi-structured interviews with justice professionals and experts, alongside documentary analysis, focusing on the 2nd Regional Labor Court (TRT-2) as a holistic case study. The study evaluates how technological innovations either broaden or restrict access to labor...

item.title: Operationalizing Organizing Vision: A Framework for Technology Intelligence
item.description:
Authors: Harold Junior Nguegang Tewamba, Alexander Chung, Mathieu Templier, Jean Robert Kala Kamdjoug

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: This study operationalizes the organizing vision (OV) within the technology intelligence process to support a mindful adoption of IT innovations. Grounded in OV theory and technology intelligence research, this study adopts the echeloned Design Science Research (eDSR) methodology. An initial iteration across the first three echelons of the eDSR was conducted through a conceptual review of 35 OV definitions. This review identified the OV content, encompassing know-what, know-why, know-how, know-when, and know-where, as the central core component of the OV concept. The resulting initial framework offers a structured approach to transforming fragmented community discourse (containing the OV) into actionable intelligence. The expected contributions include advancing the OV theory toward practical application and providing a tool to capture OV within the technology intelligence process,...

item.title: Green GenAI or, Dirty Infrastructure: Employees’ Perceived Ecological Impact Transparency of LLMs at Work
item.description:
Authors: Lutfi Al Nahin

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in abstract: pei

Abstract: Generative AI systems are increasingly embedded in knowledge work, yet their environmental consequences remain largely invisible at the point of interaction (Watson et al., 2010; Verdecchia et al., 2023), while productivity benefits are immediate and salient. This opacity creates a sustainability governance challenge: employees cannot factor environmental costs into usage decisions they cannot observe. We introduce two constructs to address this gap. Energy Disclosure Salience (EDS) captures the organizational practice of embedding ecological cost cues into LLM workflows; Perceived Ecological Impact Transparency (PEIT) captures employees’ beliefs that their organization communicates LLM environmental impacts in specific, credible, and actionable ways. Grounded in Signaling Theory and Social Learning Theory, we propose that EDS promotes pro-environmental LLM usage behavior (LLM-PEB), and...

item.title: Discursive Enactment: Exploring the Alignment Between Community and Institutional Discourses
item.description:
Authors: Harold Junior Nguegang Tewamba, Elora J Das

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in abstract: canada

Abstract: This research project aims to explore the discursive enactment of organizations in response to generative artificial intelligence by analyzing the alignment between community and institutional discourses. Drawing on organizing vision theory and IT mindfulness, we expect to propose a typology of IT mindfulness profiles based on the semantic and temporal alignment between these discourses. The methodology aims to employ dynamic topic modeling (BERTopic) to analyze data from X (Twitter) and from the official websites of each U15 Canada university between 2022 and 2026.

item.title: A Micro-governance Framework for Public-Sector Blockchain Adoption: Insights from an Ethnographic Single Case Study
item.description:
Authors: Trinh Nguyen-Phan, Raghvendra Singh

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in abstract: canadian; Canadian institution term detected

Abstract: Private blockchain adoptions are prone to failure, yet blockchain governance research has focused primarily on ecosystem-level structures rather than the micro-governance practices through which project leaders build internal legitimacy. Using an ethnographic case study of a successful Canadian public-sector-led private blockchain initiative in critical minerals mining, this study examines how leadership practices shape adoption success when leaders do not begin with superior blockchain expertise or sectoral prestige. The findings show that human-oriented values, project stewardship, and actionable optimism help build coordination capacity, collective learning, and internal credibility. Rather than treating expertise and prestige as prerequisites for success, we theorize how they can emerge through micro-governance enacted over time. The study contributes to blockchain governance and...

item.title: Exploring Structural Barriers to Education in Northern Canada: A Review & Synthesis
item.description:
Authors: Adeleine S Pascua-Matte, Suchit Ahuja, Arman Sadreddin, Rasha Ali

Source: Journal of the Association for Information Systems

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in title: canada; Canada-related term in abstract: canada, inuit

Abstract: Digital education is often promoted as a solution to educational inequities in rural, northern, and Indigenous communities in Canada and globally. However, despite increasing investments, educational outcomes in these contexts remain uneven and vulnerable to disruption. Existing digital divide research focuses primarily on accessibility to resources or skills development, offering limited insight into how educational systems build, or sustain digital capacity over time. This study conducts an integrative literature review of peer-reviewed research on digital inequality, rural broadband, Indigenous digital governance, and digital education in northern and rural Canada. With a literature review, we develop the concept of Educational Digital Capacity (EDC). The findings show how interactions among infrastructural, institutional, human, cultural, and governance capacities shape educational...

item.title: Did Recent Inflation Reflect a Nonlinear Phillips Curve?
item.description:
Authors: Paul Beaudry, Chenyu Hou, Franck Portier

Source: UCL Discovery (University College London)

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: To examine the potential role of a nonlinear Phillips curve in explaining recent US inflation, we combine evidence from both crosscity variation and aggregate time series data.A central challenge relates to how best to control for the potential confounding effect of inflation expectations.The paper explores several options.Our findings place into question the robustness that tight labour markets, due to a nonlinear Phillips curve, played a very important role in the recent inflation episode.
